skill.name: rn-auth
skill.description: Guidance for implementing authentication in Expo React Native apps, including OAuth, secure token storage, protected routes, and backend token verification.
skill.summary: Build Expo authentication flows with OAuth, secure token storage, protected routes, and backend verification.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static command execution, weak crypto, and system reconnaissance findings are false positives from Markdown backticks, authentication terminology, and Google token verification examples. The fetch and environment variable findings are legitimate documentation examples for app API calls, not executable skill behavior. No prompt injection or evidence of credential exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Examples Trigger Command Execution False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backtick and code-fence locations as Ruby or shell execution. These locations are Markdown examples and inline package names, not executable skill scripts or commands run by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are inside Markdown prose or fenced TypeScript and Python examples. I found no executable script file or instruction that runs shell commands.

low_findings.title: Network and Public Environment Examples Need App Review
low_findings.description: The guide shows an authFetch helper that reads EXPO_PUBLIC_API_URL and sends a bearer token to the configured backend. This is normal for app authentication documentation, but copied code should validate the base URL and avoid exposing tokens to untrusted endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and environment access are clear in the sample client code, but the code is documentation for a user-controlled backend. No hardcoded external host or exfiltration endpoint is present.

low_findings.title: Google Token Verification HTTP Library Finding Is Benign
low_findings.description: The Python requests.Request usage appears in a FastAPI backend example for verifying Google ID tokens. It is not system reconnaissance and does not inspect the host environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is Google OAuth token verification using google.auth transport helpers. The sample does not run discovery commands or collect local system data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Heuristic: Weak Cryptography Keyword Match
dangerous_patterns.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding points to the skill description, which discusses authentication patterns. No weak hash, cipher, or cryptographic implementation appears at that location.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains descriptive metadata for authentication guidance. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithms on that line.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Heuristic: Execution Network Credential Combination
dangerous_patterns.description: The critical combined heuristic is not confirmed. The skill is a Markdown guide with code snippets for token storage and API calls, and there is no executable payload that combines command execution, network access, and credential access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context confirms instructional examples, not runnable marketplace code. The token handling code stores and sends user app tokens only to a configured API base URL.

content.value_statement: React Native authentication often fails because OAuth redirects, token storage, and route guards must work together. This skill gives practical Expo patterns for login flows, secure storage, session restoration, and backend verification.
actual_capabilities: Explain Expo AuthSession setup for OAuth providers such as Google.
Recommend SecureStore patterns for access and refresh tokens.
Outline an AuthProvider and useAuth context structure for session state.
Show protected route logic for Expo Router authentication groups.
Describe backend Google ID token verification with a web client ID.
Help diagnose redirect URI, client ID, and token validation failures.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete runnable application.
It uses placeholder client IDs, API URLs, and user types.
It does not configure OAuth provider dashboards or native app builds.
It does not replace a full security review of backend authentication.

use_cases.title: Add OAuth Login to an Expo App
use_cases.description: Implement Google sign-in with Expo AuthSession, correct redirect handling, and secure token persistence.

use_cases.title: Connect Mobile Auth to a Backend
use_cases.description: Send bearer tokens to an API client and verify Google ID tokens with backend logic.

use_cases.title: Debug Broken Mobile Login Flows
use_cases.description: Trace redirect URI mismatches, client ID errors, expired sessions, and standalone build differences.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Login Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use rn-auth to outline a simple Expo React Native login flow with Google sign-in and secure token storage.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Auth Context
prompt_templates.prompt: Use rn-auth to review my AuthProvider design for session restore, sign-in, sign-out, loading state, and token clearing.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Redirect Problems
prompt_templates.prompt: Use rn-auth to help debug an Expo AuthSession redirect_uri_mismatch error across Expo Go and a standalone build.

prompt_templates.title: Design Mobile and Backend Verification
prompt_templates.prompt: Use rn-auth to design a secure Expo Google sign-in flow with backend ID token verification and protected Expo Router routes.

output_examples.input: I need Google sign-in in my Expo app.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step implementation plan covering AuthSession setup, client IDs, redirect handling, SecureStore token persistence, and session restoration.

output_examples.input: My login works in Expo Go but fails in a production build.
output_examples.output: A debugging checklist for redirect URI configuration, app scheme settings, OAuth client IDs, and provider console entries.

output_examples.input: How should my backend verify mobile Google login?
output_examples.output: Guidance to send the ID token to the backend, verify it with the web client ID, extract user identity, and reject invalid tokens.

best_practices: Store tokens with Expo SecureStore instead of AsyncStorage.
Validate saved tokens with the backend before restoring a session.
Use the web OAuth client ID for backend Google token verification.
anti_patterns: Do not trust a locally saved token without server validation.
Do not use an iOS client ID for backend Google token verification.
Do not copy placeholder API URLs or client IDs into production.

faq.question: Does this skill implement authentication automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that you adapt inside your Expo React Native project.

faq.question: Does it support Google sign-in?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Expo AuthSession guidance for Google OAuth and backend Google token verification.

faq.question: Does it cover Apple sign-in?
faq.answer: The description includes Apple sign-in as a use case, but the visible examples focus on Google and general OAuth patterns.

faq.question: Where should mobile tokens be stored?
faq.answer: The skill recommends Expo SecureStore for access and refresh tokens.

faq.question: Can it help with Expo Router protected routes?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to redirect unauthenticated users to an auth group and authenticated users to an app group.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples with low contextual risk and no confirmed malicious behavior.
